sanskrit: ईशा वास्यमिदं सर्वं यत्किञ्च जगत्यां जगत् । तेन त्यक्तेन भुञ्जीथा मा गृधः कस्यस्विद्धनम् ॥
transliteration: īśā vāsyam idaṁ sarvaṁ yat kiñca jagatyāṁ jagat | tena tyaktena bhuñjīthā mā gṛdhaḥ kasya svid dhanam ||
translation: All this, whatever moves in this moving world, is to be enveloped by the Lord. By that renounced, enjoy. Do not covet anyone's wealth.
meaning: From the Isha Upanishad: The entire universe is pervaded by the Divine. Enjoy what is given to you with a spirit of renunciation; do not covet what belongs to others.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: कुर्वन्नेवेह कर्माणि जिजीविषेच्छतं समाः । एवं त्वयि नान्यथेतोऽस्ति न कर्म लिप्यते नरे ॥
transliteration: kurvanneveha karmāṇi jijīviṣec chataṁ samāḥ | evaṁ tvayi nānyatheto'sti na karma lipyate nare ||
translation: Thus one should wish to live a hundred years here performing actions. There is no other way for you. Action does not cling to the person.
meaning: From the Isha Upanishad: Engage fully in righteous action while living, for action performed with the right spirit does not create binding karma.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: असुर्या नाम ते लोका अन्धेन तमसावृताः । तांस्ते प्रेत्याभिगच्छन्ति ये के चात्महनो जनाः ॥
transliteration: asuryā nāma te lokā andhena tamasāvṛtāḥ | tāṁs te pretyābhigacchanti ye ke cātmahano janāḥ ||
translation: Devilish are those worlds called, covered in blind darkness; those who kill the Self go there after death.
meaning: From the Isha Upanishad: Those who deny or kill the Self — by living entirely in ignorance — enter into dark realms of existence.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अनेजदेकं मनसो जवीयो नैनद्देवा आप्नुवन्पूर्वमर्षत् । तद्धावतोऽन्यान् अत्येति तिष्ठत् तस्मिन्नपो मातरिश्वा दधाति ॥
transliteration: anejad ekaṁ manaso javīyo nainad devā āpnuvan pūrvam arṣat | tad dhāvato'nyān atyeti tiṣṭhat tasminn apo mātariśvā dadhāti ||
translation: It is one, unmoving, faster than the mind. The senses cannot reach it; it ran ahead. Standing, it overtakes others who run. In it the Wind supports all activity.
meaning: From the Isha Upanishad: The Self is one, motionless yet faster than mind — it is beyond the reach of the senses but is the very ground in which all movement occurs.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: तदेजति तन्नैजति तद्दूरे तद्वन्तिके । तदन्तरस्य सर्वस्य तदु सर्वस्यास्य बाह्यतः ॥
transliteration: tad ejati tan naijati tad dūre tad vantike | tad antara-sya sarvasya tad u sarvasya-asya bāhyataḥ ||
translation: It moves and it moves not. It is far and it is near. It is within all this and it is also outside all this.
meaning: From the Isha Upanishad: The Brahman is the ultimate paradox — simultaneously in motion and at rest, near and far, inside all things and beyond all things.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यस्तु सर्वाणि भूतान्यात्मन्येवानुपश्यति । सर्वभूतेषु चात्मानं ततो न विजुगुप्सते ॥
transliteration: yas tu sarvāṇi bhūtāny ātmany evānupaśyati | sarva-bhūteṣu cātmānaṁ tato na vijugupsate ||
translation: He who sees all beings in his own Self and his own Self in all beings — he no longer feels revulsion (for anything).
meaning: From the Isha Upanishad: The one who perceives the Self in all beings and all beings in the Self transcends all sense of alienation and repulsion.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यस्मिन् सर्वाणि भूतानि आत्मैवाभूद्विजानतः । तत्र को मोहः कः शोक एकत्वम् अनुपश्यतः ॥
transliteration: yasmin sarvāṇi bhūtāni ātmaivābhūd vijānataḥ | tatra ko mohaḥ kaḥ śoka ekatvaṁ anupaśyataḥ ||
translation: When all beings have become one's own Self, what delusion and what sorrow can there be for the one who sees unity?
meaning: From the Isha Upanishad: In the realization that everything is the Self, all delusion and sorrow dissolve — the root cause of suffering is the illusion of separateness.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सोऽपर्यगाच्छुक्रमकायमव्रणमस्नाविरं शुद्धमपापविद्धम् । कविर्मनीषी परिभूः स्वयम्भूर्याथातथ्यतोऽर्थान् व्यदधाच्छाश्वतीभ्यः समाभ्यः ॥
transliteration: so'paryagāc chukram akāyam avraṇam asnāviraṁ śuddham apāpa-viddham | kavir manīṣī paribhūḥ svayambhūr yāthātathyato'rthān vyadadhāc chāśvatībhyaḥ samābhyaḥ ||
translation: He, the Self, encompassed all — radiant, bodiless, without scar, without sinews, pure, untouched by evil — the wise, the thought-leader, the all-pervader, the self-existent — has rightly distributed all desired objects through the eternal years.
meaning: From the Isha Upanishad: A description of Brahman as the omnipresent, pure, self-luminous consciousness that is the source of all order in the universe.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: असम्भूतिमन्धं तमः प्रविशन्ति ये तां रताः। ततो भूय इव ते तमो य उ सम्भूत्यां रताः ॥
transliteration: asambhūtim andhaṁ tamaḥ praviśanti ye tāṁ ratāḥ | tato bhūya iva te tamo ya u sambhūtyāṁ ratāḥ ||
translation: Into blind darkness enter those who worship the unmanifest. Into even greater darkness enter those who are devoted only to the manifest.
meaning: From the Isha Upanishad: Both exclusive worship of the formless and exclusive worship of the formed lead to darkness — the ideal is to understand both.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: पूषन्नेकर्षे यम सूर्य प्राजापत्य व्यूह रश्मीन् समूह तेजः। यत्ते रूपं कल्याणतमं तत्ते पश्यामि योऽसावसौ पुरुषः सोऽहमस्मि ॥
transliteration: pūṣann ekarṣe yama sūrya prājāpatya vyūha raśmīn samūha tejaḥ | yat te rūpaṁ kalyāṇatamaṁ tat te paśyāmi yo'sāv asau puruṣaḥ so'ham asmi ||
translation: O Nourisher, Solitary Seer, Controller, Sun, Child of Prajapati — spread your rays, gather your light. I see your most auspicious form. That Person who is over there — that is me.
meaning: From the Isha Upanishad: The famous prayer at the threshold of death, where the soul recognizes that the divine light and the inner self are one. 'That is me' (so'ham asmi) is a direct statement of non-dual identity.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: केनेषितं पतति प्रेषितं मनः केन प्राणः प्रथमः प्रैति युक्तः । केनेषितां वाचमिमां वदन्ति चक्षुः श्रोत्रं क उ देवो युनक्ति ॥
transliteration: keneṣitaṁ patati preṣitaṁ manaḥ kena prāṇaḥ prathamaḥ praiti yuktaḥ | keneṣitāṁ vācam imāṁ vadanti cakṣuḥ śrotraṁ ka u devo yunakti ||
translation: By whom directed does the mind fly forth? By whom enjoined does the first breath move? By whom is this speech that men speak impelled? Who is the God that sets the eye and ear to work?
meaning: From the Kena Upanishad: The profound inquiry into the ultimate subject — the witness behind perception, thought, speech, and breath. What makes the mind mind?
author: Traditional

sanskrit: श्रोत्रस्य श्रोत्रं मनसो मनो यद् वाचो ह वाचं स उ प्राणस्य प्राणः। चक्षुषश्चक्षुः अतिमुच्य धीराः प्रेत्यास्माल्लोकादमृता भवन्ति ॥
transliteration: śrotrasya śrotraṁ manaso mano yad vāco ha vācaṁ sa u prāṇasya prāṇaḥ | cakṣuṣaś cakṣuḥ atimucya dhīrāḥ pretyāsmāl lokād amṛtā bhavanti ||
translation: That which is the ear of the ear, the mind of the mind, the speech of speech, the breath of breath, the eye of the eye — the wise, having freed themselves from senses and having gone beyond this world, become immortal.
meaning: From the Kena Upanishad: Brahman is the unseen seer, the unheard hearer — the pure consciousness behind all the faculties. Knowing this, one transcends death.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यन्मनसा न मनुते येनाहुर्मनो मतम् । तदेव ब्रह्म त्वं विद्धि नेदं यदिदमुपासते ॥
transliteration: yan manasā na manute yenāhur mano matam | tad eva brahma tvaṁ viddhi nedaṁ yad idam upāsate ||
translation: That which is not thought by the mind, but by which, they say, the mind is made to think — know that alone as Brahman, not this which people worship here.
meaning: From the Kena Upanishad: Brahman is not an object of thought — it is the very ground of all thinking. What people usually worship as God is not the ultimate Brahman.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यदि मन्यसे सुवेद इति दभ्रमेवापि नूनं त्वं वेत्थ ब्रह्मणो रूपम् । यदस्य त्वं यदस्य देवेष्वथ नु मीमांस्यमेव ते मन्ये विदितम् ॥
transliteration: yadi manyase suveda iti dabhra-evāpi nūnaṁ tvaṁ vettha brahmaṇo rūpam | yad asya tvaṁ yad asya deveṣv atha nu mīmāṁsyam eva te manye viditam ||
translation: If you think 'I know Brahman well,' then surely you know very little of the form of Brahman — what concerns you and what concerns the gods. What you think you know of Brahman is still to be reflected upon.
meaning: From the Kena Upanishad: One who confidently claims to know Brahman understands little — the moment you think you fully know the Infinite, you have lost sight of it.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अप्रतिबोधविदितं मतं अमृतत्वं हि विन्दते । आत्मना विन्दते वीर्यं विद्यया विन्दते'मृतम् ॥
transliteration: apratibodha-viditaṁ mataṁ amṛtatvaṁ hi vindate | ātmanā vindate vīryaṁ vidyayā vindate'mṛtam ||
translation: By the one for whom It is not known, It is known; for the one for whom It is known, he does not know. It is not known by those who know; It is known by those who do not know.
meaning: From the Kena Upanishad: The paradox of Brahman-knowledge — the more you intellectually claim to know it, the more it eludes you; the truly wise know by unknowing.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: नायमात्मा प्रवचनेन लभ्यो न मेधया न बहुना श्रुतेन । यमेवैष वृणुते तेन लभ्यस्तस्यैष आत्मा विवृणुते तनूं स्वाम् ॥
transliteration: nāyam ātmā pravacanena labhyo na medhayā na bahunā śrutena | yam evaiṣa vṛṇute tena labhyas tasyaiṣa ātmā vivṛṇute tanūṁ svām ||
translation: This Self cannot be attained by the study of the Vedas, nor by intellect, nor by much learning. By him alone can It be attained whom the Self chooses. To him the Self reveals its own form.
meaning: From the Katha Upanishad: The ultimate Self is not the object of intellectual pursuit alone — it reveals itself to those it chooses, those who are ready and truly seeking.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: नाविरतो दुश्चरितान्नाशान्तो नासमाहितः । नाशान्तमानसो वापि प्रज्ञानेनैनमाप्नुयात् ॥
transliteration: nāvirato duścaritān nāśānto nāsamāhitaḥ | nāśānta-mānaso vāpi prajñānenainam āpnuyāt ||
translation: Not one who has not desisted from bad conduct, not one who is not tranquil, not one who is not composed, not one whose mind is not at peace — can attain this through knowledge alone.
meaning: From the Katha Upanishad: Ethical purity, mental tranquility, and self-composure are prerequisites for spiritual knowledge — intellectual cleverness alone is insufficient.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: ऊर्ध्वमूलमवाक्शाखमश्वत्थं प्राहुरव्ययम् । छन्दांसि यस्य पर्णानि यस्तं वेद स वेदवित् ॥
transliteration: ūrdhva-mūlam avāk-śākham aśvatthaṁ prāhur avyayam | chandāṁsi yasya parṇāni yas taṁ veda sa veda-vit ||
translation: The ancient Ashvattha tree has its roots above and branches below; they say it is imperishable. Its leaves are the Vedas. He who knows it knows the Vedas.
meaning: From the Katha Upanishad (also referenced in the Bhagavad Gita): The cosmic tree of existence has its roots in the transcendent and its branches in the manifest — understanding this is Vedic wisdom.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आत्मानं रथिनं विद्धि शरीरं रथमेव तु । बुद्धिं तु सारथिं विद्धि मनः प्रग्रहमेव च ॥
transliteration: ātmānaṁ rathinaṁ viddhi śarīraṁ ratham eva tu | buddhiṁ tu sārathiṁ viddhi manaḥ pragraham eva ca ||
translation: Know the Self as the lord of the chariot, and the body as the chariot. Know the intellect as the charioteer, and the mind as the reins.
meaning: From the Katha Upanishad: The famous chariot metaphor — the Self is the master, the body is the chariot, the intellect is the driver, and the mind is the reins that control the horses (the senses).
author: Traditional

sanskrit: इन्द्रियाणि हयानाहुर्विषयांस्तेषु गोचरान् । आत्मेन्द्रियमनोयुक्तं भोक्तेत्याहुर्मनीषिणः ॥
transliteration: indriyāṇi hayān āhur viṣayāṁs teṣu gocarān | ātmendriya-mano-yuktaṁ bhoktety āhur manīṣiṇaḥ ||
translation: The senses are the horses, the objects of sense are the roads. The wise call him the experiencer — joined to the self, senses, and mind.
meaning: From the Katha Upanishad: Extending the chariot metaphor — the senses are the horses, sense objects are the roads they travel, and the whole system creates the experience of the embodied soul.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यस्तु विज्ञानवान् भवति युक्तेन मनसा सदा । तस्येन्द्रियाणि वश्यानि सदश्वा इव सारथेः ॥
transliteration: yas tu vijñānavān bhavati yuktena manasā sadā | tasya indriyāṇi vaśyāni sad-aśvā iva sārateḥ ||
translation: He who has understanding, whose mind is always controlled — his senses are like the good horses of a charioteer.
meaning: From the Katha Upanishad: The person of wisdom has disciplined senses that respond to direction, like well-trained horses — they serve the master rather than running wild.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अणोरणीयान् महतो महीयान् आत्मास्य जन्तोर्निहितो गुहायाम् । तमक्रतुः पश्यति वीतशोको धातुप्रसादान्महिमानमात्मनः ॥
transliteration: aṇor aṇīyān mahato mahīyān ātmāsya jantor nihito guhāyām | tam akratuḥ paśyati vīta-śoko dhātu-prasādān mahimānam ātmanaḥ ||
translation: Smaller than the smallest, greater than the greatest — the Self is hidden in the heart of the creature. The one free from desire sees the greatness of the Self, by the grace of the Creator, becoming free from sorrow.
meaning: From the Katha Upanishad: The Self is simultaneously the smallest and the greatest — it is hidden in the heart and can only be seen by the one who has transcended selfish desire.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: न जायते म्रियते वा विपश्चिन्नायं कुतश्चिन्न बभूव कश्चित् । अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे ॥
transliteration: na jāyate mriyate vā vipaścin nāyaṁ kutaścin na babhūva kaścit | ajo nityaḥ śāśvato'yaṁ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre ||
translation: The wise Self is not born and does not die. It has not come from anywhere; it has not become anyone. Unborn, eternal, ancient — it is not slain when the body is slain.
meaning: From the Katha Upanishad (also echoed in the Bhagavad Gita): The eternal nature of the Self — it was never born and can never die, it is the deathless witness of all bodies.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: हन्ता चेन्मन्यते हन्तुं हतश्चेन्मन्यते हतम् । उभौ तौ न विजानीतो नायं हन्ति न हन्यते ॥
transliteration: hantā cen manyate hantuṁ hataś cen manyate hatam | ubhau tau na vijānīto nāyaṁ hanti na hanyate ||
translation: If the killer thinks 'I kill,' if the killed thinks 'I am killed' — both of them do not understand. This (Self) does not kill nor is it killed.
meaning: From the Katha Upanishad: The Self is beyond the duality of killer and killed — only the body can be killed; the Self is indestructible.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सर्वे वेदा यत्पदमामनन्ति तपांसि सर्वाणि च यद्वदन्ति । यदिच्छन्तो ब्रह्मचर्यं चरन्ति तत्ते पदं संग्रहेण ब्रवीम्योमित्येतत् ॥
transliteration: sarve vedā yat padam āmananti tapāṁsi sarvāṇi ca yad vadanti | yad icchanto brahmacaryyaṁ caranti tat te padaṁ saṁgraheṇa bravīmy om ity etat ||
translation: That which all the Vedas declare, which all the austerities proclaim, desiring which men practice brahmacharya — that goal I tell you briefly: it is Om.
meaning: From the Katha Upanishad: The entire spiritual enterprise of the Vedas, austerities, and celibate practice points to a single goal — the syllable Om, which is Brahman.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: किं कारणं ब्रह्म कुतः स्म जाता जीवामो केन क्व च सम्प्रतिष्ठाः । अधिष्ठिताः केन सुखेतरेषु वर्तामहे ब्रह्मविदो व्यवस्थाम् ॥
transliteration: kiṁ kāraṇaṁ brahma kutaḥ sma jātā jīvāmo kena kva ca sampratiṣṭhāḥ | adhiṣṭhitāḥ kena sukhetareṣu vartāmahe brahma-vido vyavasthām ||
translation: What is the cause — is it Brahman? From where are we born? By what do we live? Where are we established? Under whose command do we live in pleasure and pain? O knowers of Brahman, tell us the arrangement.
meaning: From the Prashna Upanishad: The opening question — the fundamental inquiry into the cause, purpose, and governance of existence.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: प्रजाकामो वाव प्रजापतिः स तपोऽतप्यत स तपस्तप्त्वा स मिथुनमुत्पादयत् रयिं च प्राणं चेत्यौ मे बहुधा प्रजाः करिष्यत इति ॥
transliteration: prajā-kāmo vāva prajāpatiḥ sa tapo'tapyata sa tapas taptvā sa mithunam utpādayat rayiṁ ca prāṇaṁ cety au me bahudhā prajāḥ kariṣyata iti ||
translation: Desirous of offspring, Prajapati practiced austerity. Having practiced austerity, he produced a pair — matter (rayi) and life-force (prana) — thinking 'these two will produce offspring for me in many ways.'
meaning: From the Prashna Upanishad: The cosmogonic teaching — all creation begins with Prajapati's austerity, producing the primordial pair of matter and life-force.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आदित्यो ह वाव प्राण उदेत्यभिप्राणन्भूतानि समुत्थापयति
transliteration: ādityo ha vāva prāṇa udety abhiprāṇann bhūtāni samutthāpayati
translation: The sun indeed is life-force. When it rises, it breathes life into beings and causes them to rise.
meaning: From the Prashna Upanishad: The cosmic dimension of prana — the sun itself is the universal life-breath that animates all beings with its rising.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: पञ्च पञ्च जना आकाशश्च महान् आत्मा च
transliteration: pañca pañca janā ākāśaś ca mahān ātmā ca
translation: Five groups of five — and space and the great Self.
meaning: From the Prashna Upanishad: Prajapati's progeny of six — the five elements, space, and the great Self — comprising the entire structure of existence.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: मनो ब्रह्मेत्युपासीत
transliteration: mano brahma ity upāsīta
translation: Meditate on mind as Brahman.
meaning: From the Prashna Upanishad: One of several progressive meditations — the mind, when understood as Brahman, becomes a vehicle for liberation.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: ब्रह्माण्डे यथा स्थितम् तथैव पिण्डे स्थितम् ॐ इत्येतदक्षरमिदं सर्वं तस्योपव्याख्यानम्
transliteration: brahmāṇḍe yathā sthitam tathaiva piṇḍe sthitam oṁ ity etad akṣaram idaṁ sarvaṁ tasya upavyākhyānam
translation: What exists in the cosmos, the same exists in the body. Om — this syllable is all this. What follows is its explanation.
meaning: From the Mundaka Upanishad: The macrocosm and the microcosm are one — and Om is the sound-symbol that comprehends and expresses this totality.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: द्वे विद्ये वेदितव्ये इति ह स्म यद् ब्रह्मविदो वदन्ति परा चैवापरा च
transliteration: dve vidye veditavye iti ha sma yad brahma-vido vadanti parā caivāparā ca
translation: Two kinds of knowledge are to be known — as the knowers of Brahman declare — higher (para) and lower (apara).
meaning: From the Mundaka Upanishad: The famous distinction between higher knowledge (direct knowledge of Brahman) and lower knowledge (all the scriptures and sciences).
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यत्तदद्रेश्यमग्राह्यमगोत्रमवर्णमचक्षुःश्रोत्रं तदपाणिपादम् ।नित्यं विभुं सर्वगतं सुसूक्ष्मं तदव्ययं यद्भूतयोनिं परिपश्यन्ति धीराः ॥
transliteration: yat tad adreśyam agrāhyam agotram avarṇam acakṣuḥ-śrotraṁ tad apāṇi-pādam | nityaṁ vibhuṁ sarva-gataṁ su-sūkṣmaṁ tad avyayam yad bhūta-yoniṁ paripaśyanti dhīrāḥ ||
translation: That which is invisible, unseizable, without origin, without qualities, without eyes or ears, without hands or feet — eternal, all-pervading, omnipresent, exceedingly subtle, undecaying — which the wise behold as the source of all beings.
meaning: From the Mundaka Upanishad: Brahman is described through negations (neti neti) — it transcends every quality and attribute yet is the ground of all.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: ब्रह्मैवेदममृतं पुरस्ताद् ब्रह्म पश्चाद् ब्रह्म दक्षिणतश्चोत्तरेण। अधश्चोर्ध्वं च प्रसृतं ब्रह्मैवेदं विश्वमिदं वरिष्ठम् ॥
transliteration: brahmaivedam amṛtaṁ purastād brahma paścād brahma dakṣiṇataś cottareṇa | adhaś cordhvaṁ ca prasṛtaṁ brahmaivedam viśvam idaṁ variṣṭham ||
translation: Brahman alone is this immortal one in front, Brahman behind, Brahman to the right and left. Brahman is spread below and above. This universe is Brahman, the best.
meaning: From the Mundaka Upanishad: Brahman is omnidirectional — above, below, in front, behind, to all sides. The entirety of the universe is Brahman.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: द्वा सुपर्णा सयुजा सखाया समानं वृक्षं परिषस्वजाते। तयोरन्यः पिप्पलं स्वाद्वत्त्यनश्नन्नन्यो अभिचाकशीति ॥
transliteration: dvā suparṇā sayujā sakhāyā samānaṁ vṛkṣaṁ pariṣasvajāte | tayor anyaḥ pippalaṁ svādv atty anaśnann anyo abhicākaśīti ||
translation: Two birds, companions always united, cling to the same tree. Of these two, one eats the sweet fruit; the other looks on without eating.
meaning: From the Mundaka Upanishad: The two-bird metaphor — the individual soul (jiva) that experiences pleasure and pain, and the pure witness consciousness (Brahman) that simply observes. Recognizing the witness is liberation.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सत्यमेव जयते नानृतं सत्येन पन्था विततो देवयानः। येनाक्रमन्त्यृषयो ह्याप्तकामा यत्र तत् सत्यस्य परमं निधानम् ॥
transliteration: satyam eva jayate nānṛtaṁ satyena panthā vitato devayānaḥ | yenākramanty ṛṣayo hy āpta-kāmā yatra tat satyasya paramaṁ nidhānam ||
translation: Truth alone conquers, not falsehood. Through truth is laid the path of the gods, by which the sages, with their desires fulfilled, go to where the supreme treasure of truth is.
meaning: From the Mundaka Upanishad: This verse — Satyameva Jayate — is the national motto of India. Truth is the supreme power and the path to the highest realization.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: तमेवं विद्वानमृत इह भवति नान्यः पन्था अयनाय विद्यते
transliteration: tam evaṁ vidvān amṛta iha bhavati nānyaḥ panthā ayanāya vidyate
translation: Knowing Him thus, the wise person becomes immortal here. There is no other path for going (to liberation).
meaning: From the Mundaka Upanishad: Knowledge of Brahman brings immortality in this very life. There is no other way to liberation.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: ओमित्येतदक्षरमिदं सर्वं तस्योपव्याख्यानम् भूतं भवत् भविष्यदिति सर्वमोंकार एव
transliteration: om ity etad akṣaram idaṁ sarvaṁ tasya upavyākhyānam bhūtaṁ bhavat bhaviṣyad iti sarvam oṁkāra eva
translation: Om — this syllable is all this. Past, present, future — all this is the syllable Om. And whatever else is beyond these three times, that also is Om.
meaning: From the Mandukya Upanishad: The opening declaration — Om encompasses all time and all existence. Everything that is, was, or will be is contained in Om.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सर्वं ह्येतद् ब्रह्म अयमात्मा ब्रह्म सोऽयमात्मा चतुष्पात्
transliteration: sarvaṁ hy etad brahma ayam ātmā brahma so'yam ātmā catuṣpāt
translation: All this is Brahman. This Self is Brahman. This Self has four quarters.
meaning: From the Mandukya Upanishad: The core teaching — all is Brahman, and the Self is Brahman. The Self is then analyzed through its four states.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: जागरितस्थानो बहिष्प्रज्ञः सप्ताङ्ग एकोनविंशतिमुखः स्थूलभुग्वैश्वानरः प्रथमः पादः
transliteration: jāgarita-sthāno bahiṣ-prajñaḥ saptāṅga ekonaviṁśati-mukhaḥ sthūla-bhug vaiśvānaraḥ prathamaḥ pādaḥ
translation: The first quarter is Vaishvanara — the waking state, outward-knowing, seven-limbed, nineteen-mouthed, experiencer of gross objects.
meaning: From the Mandukya Upanishad: The waking state (jagrit) is the first quarter of consciousness — outward-directed, experiencing the gross world through nineteen faculties (5 senses, 5 organs of action, 5 pranas, mind, intellect, ego, memory).
author: Traditional

sanskrit: स्वप्नस्थानोऽन्तःप्रज्ञः सप्ताङ्ग एकोनविंशतिमुखः प्रविविक्तभुक् तैजसो द्वितीयः पादः
transliteration: svapna-sthāno'ntaḥ-prajñaḥ saptāṅga ekonaviṁśati-mukhaḥ pravivikta-bhuk taijaso dvitīyaḥ pādaḥ
translation: The second quarter is Taijasa — the dream state, inward-knowing, seven-limbed, nineteen-mouthed, experiencer of subtle objects.
meaning: From the Mandukya Upanishad: The dream state (svapna) is the second quarter — inward-directed, experiencing subtle objects created by the mind itself.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यत्र सुप्तो न कञ्चन कामं कामयते न कञ्चन स्वप्नं पश्यति तत् सुषुप्तम् सुषुप्तस्थान एकीभूतः प्रज्ञानघन एवानन्दमयो ह्यानन्दभुक् चेतोमुखः प्राज्ञस्तृतीयः पादः
transliteration: yatra supto na kañcana kāmaṁ kāmayate na kañcana svapnaṁ paśyati tat suṣuptam suṣupta-sthāna ekībhūtaḥ prajñāna-ghana evānandamayo hy ānanda-bhuk ceto-mukhaḥ prājñas tṛtīyaḥ pādaḥ
translation: When a sleeping man desires no desires and sees no dream — that is deep sleep. The third quarter is Prajna — consolidated, a mass of consciousness, bliss-constituted, experiencer of bliss.
meaning: From the Mandukya Upanishad: Deep sleep (sushupti) is the third quarter — undivided, blissful consciousness that experiences the absence of all objects but has not yet recognized its own nature.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: एषोऽन्तर्यामी एषोऽन्तस्थ एषोऽनन्तोऽव्ययः ।अदृश्योऽग्राह्यो निर्गुणः प्रकाशो देव एकः ।तुरीयं तत्त्वम् ॥
transliteration: eṣo'ntaryāmī eṣo'ntastha eṣo'nanto'vyayaḥ | adṛśyo'grāhyo nirguṇaḥ prakāśo deva ekaḥ | turīyaṁ tattvam ||
translation: This is the inner controller, the inner dweller, this is infinite, imperishable, invisible, unseizable, without qualities, luminous, the one divine. This is the fourth — Turiya.
meaning: From the Mandukya Upanishad: The fourth state, Turiya (literally 'the fourth'), is pure consciousness itself — the witness of all three states, beyond subject and object.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: ब्रह्मविदाप्नोति परम् तदेषाऽभ्युक्ता। सत्यं ज्ञानमनन्तं ब्रह्म
transliteration: brahmavidāpnoti param tad eṣā'bhyuktā | satyaṁ jñānam anantaṁ brahma
translation: The knower of Brahman attains the supreme. On this there is this verse — Brahman is Truth, Knowledge, Infinite.
meaning: From the Taittiriya Upanishad: The famous Brahman definition — satyam, jnanam, anantam Brahma — Brahman is Truth, Consciousness, and Infinity. Knowing this, one attains liberation.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अन्नं ब्रह्मेति व्यजानात् अन्नाद्धि खल्विमानि भूतानि जायन्ते अन्नेन जातानि जीवन्ति
transliteration: annaṁ brahmeti vyajānāt annād dhi khalv imāni bhūtāni jāyante annena jātāni jīvanti
translation: He knew food as Brahman. From food indeed all beings are born; having been born, they live by food.
meaning: From the Taittiriya Upanishad: Food (anna) is Brahman — a teaching about the sacred nature of the physical world and the importance of nourishment as divine gift.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: प्राणो ब्रह्मेति व्यजानात् प्राणाद्धि खल्विमानि भूतानि जायन्ते प्राणेन जातानि जीवन्ति
transliteration: prāṇo brahmeti vyajānāt prāṇād dhi khalv imāni bhūtāni jāyante prāṇena jātāni jīvanti
translation: He knew prana as Brahman. From prana indeed all beings are born; having been born, they live by prana.
meaning: From the Taittiriya Upanishad: Each successive sheath (kosha) is a more refined understanding of Brahman — prana (life-force) is Brahman at the vital level.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: मनो ब्रह्मेत्युपासीत मनसो ह्यात्माऽन्नम् आत्मा च प्राणः आत्मा च मनः
transliteration: mano brahmety upāsīta manaso hy ātmā'nnam ātmā ca prāṇaḥ ātmā ca manaḥ
translation: Meditate on mind as Brahman. Mind is the Self of food; mind is the Self of prana; mind is the Self.
meaning: From the Taittiriya Upanishad: Mind is a higher manifestation of Brahman than food or prana — the progression of koshas (sheaths) is a journey inward toward the Self.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आनन्दो ब्रह्मेति व्यजानात् आनन्दाद्ध्येव खल्विमानि भूतानि जायन्ते आनन्देन जातानि जीवन्ति
transliteration: ānando brahmeti vyajānāt ānandād dhy eva khalv imāni bhūtāni jāyante ānandena jātāni jīvanti
translation: He knew bliss as Brahman. From bliss, all beings are born; having been born, they live by bliss.
meaning: From the Taittiriya Upanishad: The deepest layer — Brahman is bliss (ananda). All beings spring from divine joy and return to it.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: कोऽन्वस्माभ्यः प्राणयति यदेष आकाश आनन्दो न स्यात्
transliteration: ko'nv asmābhyaḥ prāṇayati yad eṣa ākāśa ānando na syāt
translation: Who indeed would breathe, who would live, if this bliss in space did not exist?
meaning: From the Taittiriya Upanishad: A profound question — the fact that we breathe, live, and function is itself proof of the ananda (bliss) of Brahman that underlies all existence.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: तस्माद्वा एतस्मादात्मन आकाशः सम्भूतः आकाशाद्वायुः वायोरग्निः अग्नेरापः अद्भ्यः पृथिवी
transliteration: tasmād vā etasmād ātmana ākāśaḥ sambhūtaḥ ākāśād vāyuḥ vāyor agniḥ agner āpaḥ adbhyaḥ pṛthivī
translation: From this Self, indeed, space arose; from space, air; from air, fire; from fire, water; from water, earth.
meaning: From the Taittiriya Upanishad: The five-fold cosmological sequence — the Self is the origin of all the elements, which progressively densify from ether to earth.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सह नाववतु सह नौ भुनक्तु सह वीर्यं करवावहै। तेजस्विनावधीतमस्तु मा विद्विषावहै ॥ ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः ॥
transliteration: saha nāv avatu saha nau bhunaktu saha vīryaṁ karavāvahai | tejasvinav adhītam astu mā vidviṣāvahai || oṁ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ ||
translation: May He protect us both. May He nourish us both. May we work together with great vigor. May our study be illuminating. May we not hate each other. Om Peace, Peace, Peace.
meaning: From the Taittiriya Upanishad: The shanti patha — the invocation of peace between teacher and student. This prayer for mutual protection, nourishment, vigor, and illumined study is still chanted before Upanishadic study.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आत्मा वा इदमेक एवाग्र आसीत् नान्यत्किंचन मिषत् स ईक्षत लोकान्नु सृजा इति
transliteration: ātmā vā idam eka evāgra āsīt nānyat kiñcana miṣat sa īkṣata lokān nu sṛjā iti
translation: In the beginning, this was the Self alone, nothing else winked. He thought: 'Let me now create the worlds.'
meaning: From the Aitareya Upanishad: The creation account — in the beginning there was only the Self (Atman), and from an act of consciousness (thinking/seeing), the worlds came into being.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: स एतान् लोकानसृजत अम्भो मरीचिर्मरमापोऽदो
transliteration: sa etān lokān asṛjata ambho marīcir maram āpo'do
translation: He created these worlds: water, light-rays, earth, and the waters below.
meaning: From the Aitareya Upanishad: The first act of creation — the primordial Self creates the basic cosmological realms.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: प्रज्ञानं ब्रह्म
transliteration: prajñānaṁ brahma
translation: Consciousness is Brahman.
meaning: From the Aitareya Upanishad: One of the four Mahavakyas (Great Sayings) of the Upanishads — Brahman is not a thing or a person but pure, undivided Consciousness itself.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: कोऽयमात्मा इति वयमुपासीमहि कतर आत्मा
transliteration: ko'yam ātmā iti vayam upāsīmahi katara ātmā
translation: We worship the Self — but which Self?
meaning: From the Aitareya Upanishad: The sincere seeker's question — not just the fact of the Self but the nature of which Self is the true one.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: येन विजानाति रूपं रसं गन्धं शब्दांस्पर्शान् स एष आत्मा
transliteration: yena vijānāti rūpaṁ rasaṁ gandhaṁ śabdāṁs tarśān sa eṣa ātmā
translation: That by which one perceives form, taste, smell, sound, and touch — that is the Self.
meaning: From the Aitareya Upanishad: The Self is the pure knowing behind all perception — not the senses themselves but the consciousness that knows through them.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: ओम् इत्येतदक्षरमुद्गीथमुपासीत
transliteration: oṁ ity etad akṣaram udgītham upāsīta
translation: Meditate on Om as the Udgitha (the sacred chant).
meaning: From the Chandogya Upanishad: Om is the Udgitha — the high chant in the Sama Veda ritual. Meditating on Om is meditating on the essence of all chant and all existence.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: एतत् साम गायन्नास्ते एतत् साम गायन्नास्ते एतत् साम गायन्नास्ते
transliteration: etat sāma gāyann āste etat sāma gāyann āste etat sāma gāyann āste
translation: Singing this Saman, he sits; singing this Saman, he sits; singing this Saman, he sits.
meaning: From the Chandogya Upanishad: The threefold repetition emphasizes the power of the Saman chant — the repetition itself is a form of meditation.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: उद्गीथं गीयते एतत् उत् च तत् गानं च इति
transliteration: udgīthaṁ gīyate etat ut ca tat gānaṁ ca iti
translation: The Udgitha is sung upward — 'ud' means upward and 'githa' means chant.
meaning: From the Chandogya Upanishad: Etymology reveals metaphysics — the upward movement of sacred chant (Udgitha) corresponds to the upward aspiration of the soul toward Brahman.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: तत्त्वमसि श्वेतकेतो
transliteration: tat tvam asi śvetaketo
translation: Thou art That, Shvetaketu.
meaning: From the Chandogya Upanishad: The most famous of the four Mahavakyas — 'That thou art.' The essence of the universe (Brahman) and your innermost Self are one and the same.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: स य एषोऽणिमैतदात्म्यमिदं सर्वं तत् सत्यं स आत्मा तत्त्वमसि श्वेतकेतो
transliteration: sa ya eṣo'ṇimaitadātmyam idaṁ sarvaṁ tat satyaṁ sa ātmā tat tvam asi śvetaketo
translation: That which is this subtle essence — all this has That as its Self. That is the real. That is the Self. Thou art That, Shvetaketu.
meaning: From the Chandogya Upanishad: The repeated teaching of Uddalaka to his son — the subtle essence pervading the universe is the same as the innermost Self of every being.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आत्मैवेदं सर्वं
transliteration: ātmaivedam sarvaṁ
translation: All this is indeed the Self.
meaning: From the Chandogya Upanishad: The non-dual proclamation — the entirety of existence is nothing other than the Self (Atman/Brahman).
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सर्वं खल्विदं ब्रह्म तज्जलानिति शान्त उपासीत
transliteration: sarvaṁ khalv idaṁ brahma taj-jalān iti śānta upāsīta
translation: All this is indeed Brahman — from which it is born, into which it dissolves, and by which it breathes. Meditatively worship this with a calm mind.
meaning: From the Chandogya Upanishad: One of the great declarations — Sarvam khalvidam brahma. Everything is Brahman — its source, sustainer, and dissolution. Worship it with a peaceful mind.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अन्तश्चरति भूतेषु गुहायां विश्वमूर्तिधृक्
transliteration: antaś carati bhūteṣu guhāyāṁ viśva-mūrti-dhṛk
translation: It moves within beings, in the cave of the heart, bearing all forms.
meaning: From the Chandogya Upanishad: Brahman moves within all creatures, hidden in the innermost cave of the heart, wearing the forms of the entire universe.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: एकचक्रं वर्तते देवमेकम् एको अग्निर्बहुधा समिद्धः
transliteration: eka-cakraṁ vartate devam ekam eko agnir bahudhā samiddhaḥ
translation: The one divine moves with one wheel; one fire is kindled in many places.
meaning: From the Chandogya Upanishad: The one Brahman is like a single fire that burns in many hearths — the multiplicity of the world comes from the one divine ground.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यथाग्नेः क्षुद्रा विस्फुलिङ्गा व्युच्चरन्त्येवमेवास्मादात्मनः सर्वे प्राणाः
transliteration: yathāgneḥ kṣudrā visphuliṅgā vyuccaranty evam evāsmād ātmanaḥ sarve prāṇāḥ
translation: Just as from a fire small sparks fly forth, so from this Self all pranas (life-forces) arise.
meaning: From the Chandogya Upanishad: All life-energies are sparks from the fire of the universal Self — we are not separate from the divine but expressions of it.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: ब्रह्म वा इदमग्र आसीत् तदात्मानमेवावेत् अहं ब्रह्मास्मीति
transliteration: brahma vā idam agra āsīt tad ātmānam evāvet ahaṁ brahmāsmīti
translation: In the beginning, this was Brahman alone. It knew itself: 'I am Brahman.'
meaning: From the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad: The very first act was consciousness recognizing itself — and this self-recognition ('Aham Brahmasmi') is still the highest spiritual act available to any being.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अहं ब्रह्मास्मि
transliteration: ahaṁ brahmāsmi
translation: I am Brahman.
meaning: From the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad: The third Mahavakya — 'Aham Brahmasmi.' The direct declaration of non-dual identity: the individual I (aham) is none other than the infinite Brahman.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: नेति नेति
transliteration: neti neti
translation: Not this, not this.
meaning: From the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad: The method of Yajnavalkya — Brahman cannot be grasped by any positive description. Every concept, every quality, must be negated until only pure, undifferentiated awareness remains.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: कस्य कामाय पुत्रः प्रियो भवति कस्य कामाय वित्तं प्रियं भवति
transliteration: kasya kāmāya putraḥ priyo bhavati kasya kāmāya vittaṁ priyaṁ bhavati
translation: For whose love is a son dear? For whose love is wealth dear?
meaning: From the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad: Yajnavalkya to Maitreyi — the great question about what makes anything lovable. The answer is: the Self within all things is why they are dear.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आत्मा वा अरे द्रष्टव्यः श्रोतव्यो मन्तव्यो निदिध्यासितव्यः
transliteration: ātmā vā are draṣṭavyaḥ śrotavyo mantavyo nididhyāsitavyaḥ
translation: The Self is to be seen, to be heard, to be reflected upon, to be meditated upon.
meaning: From the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad: Yajnavalkya's four-stage path — hearing (shravana), reflection (manana), and deep meditation (nididhyasana) on the Self are the complete spiritual methodology.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सर्वस्य चात्मा भवति यदा एतमात्मानं अनुपश्यति
transliteration: sarvasya cātmā bhavati yadā etam ātmānaṁ anupaśyati
translation: One becomes the Self of all when one sees this Self.
meaning: From the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad: The fruit of Self-realization is the expansion of identity to include all beings — the realized soul becomes the Self of everything.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यत्र हि द्वैतमिव भवति तदितर इतरं पश्यति यत्र त्वस्य सर्वमात्मैवाभूत् तत्केन कं पश्येत्
transliteration: yatra hi dvaitam iva bhavati tad itara itaraṁ paśyati yatra tv asya sarvam ātmaivābhūt tat kena kaṁ paśyet
translation: Where there is something like duality, one sees another. But when everything has become the Self alone, by whom and whom does one see?
meaning: From the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad: In ultimate non-duality, all perceiving dissolves — there is no longer a seer and a seen, only the Self aware of itself.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: विज्ञानमानन्दं ब्रह्म
transliteration: vijñānam ānandaṁ brahma
translation: Brahman is consciousness and bliss.
meaning: From the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad: Brahman is characterized as pure awareness (vijnana) and infinite bliss (ananda) — the conscious and blissful ground of all existence.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: असतो मा सद्गमय। तमसो मा ज्योतिर्गमय। मृत्योर्मामृतं गमय ॥ ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः ॥
transliteration: asato mā sad gamaya | tamaso mā jyotir gamaya | mṛtyor mā amṛtaṁ gamaya || oṁ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ ||
translation: Lead me from the unreal to the real. Lead me from darkness to light. Lead me from death to immortality. Om Peace, Peace, Peace.
meaning: From the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad: Perhaps the most beloved prayer from the Upanishads — a three-fold petition for the spiritual journey from illusion to truth, from ignorance to wisdom, from mortality to eternal life.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: पूर्णमदः पूर्णमिदं पूर्णात् पूर्णमुदच्यते। पूर्णस्य पूर्णमादाय पूर्णमेवावशिष्यते ॥
transliteration: pūrṇam adaḥ pūrṇam idaṁ pūrṇāt pūrṇam udacyate | pūrṇasya pūrṇam ādāya pūrṇam evāvaśiṣyate ||
translation: That is fullness. This is fullness. Fullness arises from fullness. When fullness is taken from fullness, fullness still remains.
meaning: From the Brihadaranyaka and Isha Upanishads: The supreme declaration of infinite plenitude — Brahman is infinite, the universe is infinite, and even when infinity is taken from infinity, infinity remains. The infinite cannot be diminished.
author: Traditional
